extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a lab‑scale gantry crane – a small, hydraulically‑driven gantry that mimics the harbor cranes of the Port of Antwerp. It transports lightweight pucks that represent shipping containers between predefined locations. The hardware comprises the crane structure, motorised axes, and an embedded controller accessed via a Python‑based interface. The system can run autonomously without a twin, but is deliberately kept simple, open‑source and documented on GitHub to serve research and education in logistics, cyber‑physical systems and control engineering. Its modest size, deterministic kinematics and the availability of CAD and kinematic models make it a tractable platform for digital‑twin experimentation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides several dedicated services: (1) a Trajectory Generator Service that uses the crane’s kinematic model to compute swing‑free optimal movement trajectories; (2) a Simulation Service that emulates the crane and its controllers, enabling prediction and “what‑if” analysis of generated trajectories; (3) a Continuous Validation Service that continuously compares real‑time sensor measurements of executed trajectories with the simulated predictions, computes distance metrics, checks them against a configurable threshold and raises alerts on the Grafana dashboard; (4) a Logging Service that records the crane’s state in a TimescaleDB time‑series database for historical analysis; (5) a Grafana Dashboard for data visualization, trajectory monitoring and operator alerts; (6) an MQTT Broker that routes signalling between the above services. The primary users are the crane operator (who monitors, configures and receives alerts) and researchers (who experiment with control, validation and fault‑diagnosis algorithms).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin is built with open‑source tooling and containerisation: Python applications implement the Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application and Human‑Machine Interface; all services run as Docker containers orchestrated via a single Docker‑Compose command. Data storage uses TimescaleDB (a PostgreSQL‑based time‑series database) and visualisation relies on Grafana. Inter‑service communication employs an MQTT broker (standard MQTT protocol). The Trajectory Generator leverages a mathematically‑derived kinematic model of the crane, while the Simulation Service uses a software simulation of the crane dynamics (implementation details are not further specified). The overall architecture is documented with the C4 modelling approach, and the complete source code and documentation are publicly hosted on GitHub.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The gantry crane exemplar implements a single digital‑twin instance that is built from six logical containers – a TimescaleDB database, a Grafana dashboard, a Trajectory Generator Service, a Simulation Service, a Continuous Validation Service, and an MQTT broker. The physical side consists of the Physical Crane, a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface, a Control Application, a Logging Application and a Human‑Machine Interface. The digital‑twin containers are hierarchically organised: data are stored in the database, visualised by the dashboard, and the services (trajectory generation, simulation and validation) interact through the MQTT broker. Coordination is therefore centralised within this single twin, with the broker mediating communication among the constituent services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a service‑oriented architecture orchestrated by Docker‑Compose. All six digital‑twin containers are launched together, sharing a Docker network. The MQTT broker provides publish/subscribe messaging that synchronises the Trajectory Generator, Simulation, and Continuous Validation services. The TimescaleDB stores time‑series data accessed by the Logging Application and Grafana for visualisation. Interaction between services occurs via MQTT messages and standard SQL queries to the database, constituting a loosely coupled yet centrally coordinated constellation of components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: External integration is realised through standard protocols: MQTT is used for inter‑service signalling, and the TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL‑compatible) is accessed via SQL by the Logging Application and Grafana dashboard. No explicit links to other enterprise systems or external digital twins are described, but the use of widely adopted protocols (MQTT, SQL) enables straightforward extension to outside systems that support these standards.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin comprises a geometric CAD model of the gantry crane, an analytical kinematics model used by the Trajectory Generator Service to compute swing‑free movement trajectories, and a behavioral simulation model of the crane and its low‑level controllers employed by the Simulation Service. All runtime data (e.g., joint positions, velocities, controller states) are continuously recorded by the Logging Application and persisted in a TimescaleDB instance. The database serves as the single source of truth for the Grafana Dashboard and for the Continuous Validation Service. Model relationships: the kinematic model feeds the trajectory generator, the same model underlies the simulation, and both are used by the validation service to compare measured trajectories against simulated ones. Data exchange between services uses an MQTT broker.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by the motorised drives of the physical gantry crane (trolley and hoist). Commands are issued from the Control Application, which invokes the Python‑based Physical Crane Interface to communicate with the crane’s controllers. The interface supports commands for motion, homing and zeroing of the crane. Actuation range covers the full travel limits of the crane as defined by its mechanical design.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The crane’s controllers expose the current state (position, velocity and status flags) which is read by the Logging Application. This state information constitutes the sensing infrastructure; no external sensors are explicitly described. State updates are streamed to the Logging Application at a frequency sufficient for continuous validation (exact sampling rate not specified) and transmitted via the Python interface and MQTT broker to be stored in TimescaleDB.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by continuously validating measured trajectories against the simulation output. The Continuous Validation Service computes distance metrics between the real‑world execution and the simulated trajectory, compares them to a configurable threshold, and raises alerts via the Grafana dashboard if the deviation exceeds the limit. Validation thus detects model drift, sensor noise or controller faults. Uncertainty sources include measurement noise from the crane controllers and simplifications in the kinematic and simulation models. The threshold provides a quantitative bound on acceptable error, serving as a quality‑assurance mechanism for both models and data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The physical crane is equipped with a swing‑angle encoder, an anemometer and motor controllers whose measurements are collected by an Arduino board. The Arduino code (stored in the repository under the arduino folder) reads the sensor values and makes them available to the PC. A Python program called the Physical Crane Interface connects to the Arduino‑controlled hardware and continuously provides the current crane state (position, swing angle, wind speed, motor status) to the Logging Application. The Logging Application records all received state data in a TimescaleDB time‑series database. The Continuous Validation Service later reads these logged measurements from the database, compares them with simulated trajectories generated by the Simulation Service, and computes distance metrics. Data integration between services is performed via the shared TimescaleDB instance and via an MQTT broker for signalling, but the exact sampling frequency or preprocessing steps are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital twin provides control commands through several services. The Trajectory Generator Service uses a kinematic model of the crane to compute optimal movement trajectories that end with zero residual swing. These trajectory set‑points are sent to the Control Application, which uses the Physical Crane Interface (a Python program) to transmit the commands to the crane’s motor controllers. The Human Machine Interface (a Python front‑end) allows an operator to manually configure and trigger commands, which are likewise routed through the Control Application to the physical hardware. No explicit command message format, authentication, or conflict‑resolution mechanisms are described in the documents; confirmation of command execution is implied by the Logging Application’s continuous state logging.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑service communication within the digital twin relies on an MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto) that handles signalling between the Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation and other services. All services are packaged as Docker containers (Database, MQTT broker, Dashboard, and the Python services) and are orchestrated with a Docker‑compose file, creating a local container network (typically the default bridge network). The documents do not detail bandwidth, latency, or redundancy requirements, nor do they specify additional security protocols beyond the choice of an open‑source MQTT broker.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The entire system is deployed on a single personal computer (on‑premise). The physical‑side software runs as native Python processes, while the digital‑twin components (TimescaleDB, Grafana dashboard, and MQTT broker) are containerised with Docker. Deployment is automated via a Docker‑compose file that brings up the database, broker and dashboard with a single command and also performs first‑time initialization of the database and dashboard. The required infrastructure therefore includes a PC capable of running Docker and Python, sufficient storage for time‑series data in TimescaleDB, and modest compute resources for the simulation and validation services. Scalability is implied by the containerised architecture (additional instances could be started), but no explicit cloud or edge hosting, high‑availability, or redundancy mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The gantry‑crane digital twin follows a four‑stage lifecycle described in the papers: (1) Development – creation of CAD geometry and a kinematic model of the crane, packaged as Python modules; (2) Integration – the models are wrapped as services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) and deployed together with supporting components (TimescaleDB, Grafana, MQTT broker) via a single Docker‑compose command; (3) Operation – the running services ingest real‑time state from the Physical Crane Interface, log data to TimescaleDB, generate optimal trajectories, simulate them and continuously validate measured vs simulated trajectories; (4) Evolution – the exemplar is reused in successive research projects (continuous‑validation methods, fault‑detection, DarTwin notation) where new services or model refinements are added and redeployed. Transition between stages is managed by the Docker‑compose orchestration; lifecycle governance is informal and relies on the open‑source GitHub repository for version control and documentation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering of the gantry‑crane twin is performed with Python applications and containerisation. The physical‑crane interface, control and logging applications are written in Python and communicate through an MQTT broker. The digital twin consists of six Docker containers: TimescaleDB, Grafana, Trajectory‑Generator (kinematics‑based optimisation), Simulation, Continuous‑Validation and the MQTT broker. Development is tracked in a public GitHub repository, providing version control. Quality assurance is achieved by the Continuous‑Validation Service, which continuously compares measured trajectories with simulated ones, computes distance metrics and raises alerts when a configurable threshold is exceeded. Validation is further supported by the Simulation Service that can reproduce generated trajectories. Evolution management is demonstrated by successive publications that extend the twin (e.g., adding fault‑detection classifiers, applying the DarTwin notation) and by the ability to redeploy updated containers via Docker‑compose.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides decision support through three main analytics services. The Trajectory Generator creates optimal, swing‑free motion plans that are shown on the Grafana dashboard. The Simulation Service produces predicted sensor traces for those plans. The Continuous‑Validation Service calculates quantitative distance metrics between the predicted and the actual sensor data, compares them to a predefined threshold and visualises the result as confidence bounds (blue/yellow bands) on the Grafana dashboard. When the threshold is violated, operators receive visual alerts in the Human‑Machine Interface and can intervene via the Python front‑end to re‑plan or halt the crane. Thus the twin supplies both prescriptive insight (optimal trajectories) and diagnostic insight (validation alerts with confidence measures).

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.

Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

are controls for the crane’s movement, homing and zeroing,
the real-time status and configurations of the logger w.r.t
database writeout. Figure 7 shows that with the single docker

Fig. 8. Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces.



_A. Usage in Publications_


The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper
itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying
poster used the crane as illustrative example.
Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous
validation through the reuse of existing model validation
techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was
developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more
detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we
refer the reader to this publication.
The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored
DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified
multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one
hand the development process of the crane was used to derive
those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple
future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the
DarTwin notation.

The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we
explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques
to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended
the continuous validation method of [5] with classification
techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar.


_B. Usage in Teaching_


At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended
Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based
Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the
exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to
an entire harbor system, with a container identification system
to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with
a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading
order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of
academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will
be revisited in the second semester.

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

_A. Usage in Publications_

multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one

future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the

explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques
to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended
the continuous validation method of [5] with classification

Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based
Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the
exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to
an entire harbor system, with a container identification system
to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with
a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading
order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of
academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will
be revisited in the second semester.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and

_A. Architecture_

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give



an overview of the components powering it. We should note
that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation
of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram.
Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity
and readability only the most necessary relationships are
shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the
hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane.
Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the
cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An
**Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers,
a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of
the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An
**Arduino** measures and controls those last three components.
Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane.


_B. Implementation_


Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the
operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug
in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of
the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution
of the C4 Containers visually.
For the implementation, we bundled all the Python C4
Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use
this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it
remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital
twin services.

For ease of deployment, the **Database**, **MQTT Broker** and
**Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A

docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and
down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of
the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically
handled by this docker compose file.


Furthermore some of the components warrant a small explanation for their implementation choices. For the database,
the choice for TimescaleDB was driven by three factors: (i)
it’s based on PostgreSQL, allowing for commonly known SQL
queries for access. (ii) it’s optimized for time series data, which
is what is logged. (iii) it’s open source. For the dashboard, the
choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support
for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source.
Lastly, for the MQTT Broker, the ubiquitous and open-source
Eclipse Mosquitto was used.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium

_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

_A. Usage in Publications_


The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper
itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying
poster used the crane as illustrative example.
Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous
validation through the reuse of existing model validation
techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was
developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more
detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we
refer the reader to this publication.
The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored
DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified
multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one
hand the development process of the crane was used to derive
those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple
future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the
DarTwin notation.

The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we
explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques
to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended
the continuous validation method of [5] with classification
techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar.


_B. Usage in Teaching_


At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended
Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based
Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the
exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to
an entire harbor system, with a container identification system
to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with
a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading
order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of
academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will
be revisited in the second semester.

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.
